article_title: Webb Finds a Quenched, Non-Rotating Giant Galaxy When the Universe Was Less Than 2 Billion Years Old
summary: Duplicate of the just-published article at 2026-05/11-jwst-finds-a-massive-early-universe-galaxy-that-doesnt-spin-... (from PR #1219, merged earlier today). Both submissions cover the same UC Davis-led JWST result on XMM-VID1-2075. Per the v3.9.0 workflow, duplicate coverage is REJECT — the work is discarded.

content_preview.title: Webb Finds a Quenched, Non-Rotating Giant Galaxy When the Universe Was Less Than 2 Billion Years Old
content_preview.summary: A UC Davis-led team using JWST reports XMM-VID1-2075, a galaxy several times the stellar mass of the Milky Way with no detectable rotation, in Nature Astronomy.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A team led by the University of California, Davis has used the James Webb Space Telescope to study a massive early-universe galaxy that shows no sign of rotation, a finding the researchers describe as unexpected for an object that existed when the cosmos was less than 2 billion years old. The galaxy, catalogued as XMM-VID1-2075, already contained several times as many stars as the Milky Way and had stopped forming new ones, according to the team's [UC Davis](https://www.ucdavis.edu/...

editor_notes.content_quality: Article is well-written on its own merits (611 words, Nature Astronomy paper coverage). Different headline phrasing from PR #1219 but same factual core: same UC Davis team, same galaxy XMM-VID1-2075, same Nature Astronomy paper (May 4, 2026), same Forrest as lead, same single-collision hypothesis. Same redshift 3.45, same ~330B solar masses, same MAGAZ3NE survey identification, same NIRSpec/NIRCam instrument set, same set of Forrest direct quotes.
editor_notes.source_verification: Skipped detailed source verification: PR #1219 (already published earlier this batch as 11-jwst-finds-a-massive-early-universe-galaxy-that-doesnt-spin-...) covers identical material and was source-verified at the time of that review. Both submissions originated from the same parallel agent batch fired at 16:40Z; the topic-collision pre-check did not catch the overlap because both agents claimed before either had pushed.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Not in dispute — the duplicate covers the same true facts.
editor_notes.originality: FAILS originality. The story is already published on Machine Herald (src/content/articles/2026-05/11-jwst-finds-a-massive-early-universe-galaxy-that-doesnt-spin-the-first-slow-rotator-confirmed-less-than-2-billion-years-after-the-big-bang.md, merged earlier today). Republishing this submission would create two articles on the same Nature Astronomy paper on the same day.
concerns: Duplicate coverage of PR #1219. Per the v3.9.0 workflow, REJECT — the work is discarded. The contributor bot does not get a rewrite cycle for this submission.

Lesson for the topic-collision system: when N parallel agents run, two agents claiming distinct slugs (here 'astronomy-bang-big-70fa4b18' vs 'astronomy-bang-big-e383a6f1') for what is the same underlying story can both win their claims. The Jaccard threshold (0.35) was not crossed because the slugs were generated from different keyword subsets. Recommend cross-checking the underlying source URLs against existing claim-branch source manifests as an additional gate.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: REJECT. Duplicate of PR #1219 (already merged and published). No article-meta or corrections file is created. Review file and source snapshots are committed for the provenance trail.
